patterns.name: Visa card number
patterns.description: Visa primary account number, 13 or 16 digits starting with 4. Validate with Luhn to suppress random digit strings.
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: Any 13/16-digit string starting with 4 matches; Luhn validation is essential. Strip spaces/dashes before matching or add a separator-tolerant variant. Order/invoice numbers of the same length can collide pre-validation.

patterns.name: Mastercard card number
patterns.description: Mastercard 16-digit PAN covering the legacy 51-55 BIN range and the 2221-2720 range. Luhn validation required.
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: Requires Luhn to avoid arbitrary 16-digit numbers. The 2-series alternative is bounded to the official 222100-272099 range to limit false positives.

patterns.name: American Express card number
patterns.description: American Express 15-digit PAN starting with 34 or 37.
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: 15-digit numbers starting 34/37 are uncommon outside Amex but Luhn validation is still required to reduce noise.

patterns.name: Discover card number
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: Overlaps with some UnionPay/Maestro ranges (6xxx). Luhn validation required; BIN database lookup recommended for network attribution.

patterns.name: Diners Club card number
patterns.description: Diners Club 14-digit PAN (300-305, 36, 38-39).
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: 14-digit numbers; the 36/38 prefixes can overlap other schemes. Luhn validation required.

patterns.name: JCB card number
patterns.description: JCB 16-digit PAN (3528-3589) plus legacy 2131/1800 15-digit variants.
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: The 1800 prefix can collide with phone-number fragments; Luhn validation required.

patterns.name: Credit card (separator-tolerant)
patterns.description: Generic primary account number allowing spaces/hyphens between digit groups; gated by the Luhn checksum. Catches PANs pasted in human-readable 4-4-4-4 form that the contiguous per-network rules miss.
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: Matches any 13-19 digit run with optional single separators; Luhn validation is load-bearing. Lower confidence than per-network rules so those win when both fire.

patterns.name: IBAN (International Bank Account Number)
patterns.description: Generic IBAN: 2-letter country code, 2 check digits, then up to 30 BBAN chars. Validate with mod-97 (ISO 7064) and per-country length.
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: Tolerates spaces/hyphens between groups (the human-readable form); the iban_mod97 validator compacts separators before the checksum, so false positives are still suppressed.

patterns.name: SWIFT/BIC code
patterns.description: SWIFT business identifier code: 4-char bank, 2-char ISO country, 2-char location, optional 3-char branch (8 or 11 chars).
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: 8/11-char uppercase alphanumeric tokens are common (env vars, IDs). Validating the 5th-6th chars against ISO-3166 country codes greatly reduces false positives.

patterns.name: US ABA routing number
patterns.description: US ABA/RTN 9-digit routing transit number, leading two digits restricted to valid Federal Reserve district ranges. Validate with the 3-7-1 weighted checksum.
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: Any 9-digit number can collide (SSN without dashes, NPI, order ids). The leading-digit range and aba_routing checksum together are required to be usable.

patterns.name: US Social Security Number
patterns.description: US SSN in dashed format AAA-GG-SSSS, excluding invalid area (000, 666, 900-999), group (00), and serial (0000) blocks.
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: Dashed form is reliable. Undashed 9-digit SSNs collide with routing/NPI numbers and are intentionally excluded here; use a context-gated variant for those. Does not match SSNs written with spaces.

patterns.name: US ITIN
patterns.description: US Individual Taxpayer Identification Number: starts with 9, group digits in IRS-assigned ranges (50-65, 70-88, 90-92, 94-99).
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: Shares the SSN dashed format; the leading 9 plus restricted group ranges distinguish ITINs. Undashed forms not matched.

patterns.name: US passport number
patterns.description: US passport book number: 9 digits (older) or one letter followed by 8 digits (newer next-gen books).
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: Very low specificity; 8-9 digit numbers are everywhere. Only use with strong context keywords (passport, document number) nearby. High false-positive rate standalone.

patterns.name: Korean Resident Registration Number (주민등록번호)
patterns.description: South Korean RRN YYMMDD-SXXXXXXC: 6-digit birthdate, gender/century digit 1-4 for nationals, then serial and check digit. Validate with the standard mod-11 weighted checksum.
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: Embedded date validation plus kr_rrn_checksum required; otherwise any 13-digit dashed number matches. Note RRNs are frequently masked as 900101-1******.

patterns.name: Korean Foreigner Registration Number (외국인등록번호)
patterns.description: South Korean Alien/Foreigner Registration Number, identical structure to the RRN but with gender/century digit 5-8.
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: Same checksum (kr_rrn_checksum) as RRN. Distinguished only by the 5-8 leading digit of the second group; embedded date validation reduces collisions.

patterns.name: UK National Insurance Number
patterns.description: UK NINO: two prefix letters (excluding disallowed letters D, F, I, Q, U, V and the invalid second letter O), six digits, one suffix letter A-D.
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: Disallowed prefixes (BG, GB, KN, NK, NT, TN, ZZ) are not excluded here and should be filtered in post. Often written with spaces (AB 12 34 56 C); add a separator-tolerant variant if needed.

patterns.name: German ID card / tax ID (11-digit IdNr)
patterns.description: German Steuerliche Identifikationsnummer (Steuer-IdNr): 11 digits, first digit non-zero.
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: Plain 11-digit numbers collide with many identifiers (NPI is 10, phone numbers, order ids). Use with German-language context keywords. The official IdNr has an ISO 7064 mod-11,10 check digit that should be validated downstream.

patterns.name: French INSEE / social security number
patterns.description: French NIR/INSEE number: sex digit (1/2), 2-digit birth year, 2-digit month, department (with Corsica 2A/2B), commune and order, plus 2-digit key.
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: 15 digits with optional A/B for Corsica. Includes a mod-97 control key (last 2 digits) that should be validated downstream to cut false positives. Often spaced into groups.

patterns.name: Spanish DNI/NIF
patterns.description: Spanish DNI/NIF: 8 digits followed by a control letter (excluding I, O, U and other unused letters).
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: The control letter is derived as digits mod 23; validate the letter against that table to remove most false positives. NIE (foreigner) numbers start with X/Y/Z and are not matched here.

patterns.name: Italian Codice Fiscale
patterns.description: Italian tax code: 6 surname/name letters, 2 year digits, month letter, 2 day digits (40+ for female), area code, and a control letter.
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: Highly structured so low false-positive rate, but the final check character should be validated with the official algorithm for certainty.

patterns.name: AWS Access Key ID
patterns.description: AWS access key identifier: a known 4-char type prefix (AKIA long-term, ASIA temporary, AROA role, etc.) followed by 16 base32 uppercase chars.
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: Very specific; near-zero false positives. ASIA prefix denotes temporary STS credentials which must be paired with a session token to be usable.

patterns.name: AWS Secret Access Key
patterns.description: AWS secret access key: 40-char base64-ish string, detected when near an 'aws' context token and quoted. Confirm with Shannon entropy.
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: Any quoted 40-char base64 string near 'aws' matches; entropy gating is essential because base64 hashes/IDs of the same length collide. A context-free 40-char variant would be far too noisy.

patterns.name: AWS session token
patterns.description: AWS STS session token: long base64 blob beginning with the recognizable 'FwoGZXIvYXdzE' header.
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: The fixed header makes this very specific. Real tokens are several hundred chars; the 100+ length floor avoids matching the header alone.

patterns.name: GCP service account key (JSON)
patterns.description: Google Cloud service-account key file marker: the JSON field type:service_account, which accompanies a private_key block.
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: Matches the JSON marker, not the key itself; pair with a PEM private-key detector and the client_email field to confirm a real credential file. Documentation snippets can match.

patterns.name: GCP OAuth2 access token (ya29)
patterns.description: Google OAuth2 / GCP access token beginning with the ya29. prefix.
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: Prefix is distinctive. These are short-lived but still sensitive in logs. Lower-bound length avoids matching the bare prefix.

patterns.name: Google API key (AIza)
patterns.description: Google/Firebase API key: fixed AIza prefix plus 35 url-safe base64 characters (39 total).
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: Highly specific prefix and fixed length yield very few false positives.

patterns.name: Azure Storage connection string
patterns.description: Azure Storage account connection string containing AccountName and a base64 AccountKey (typically 88 chars).
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: Distinctive literal structure; near-zero false positives. The full key is usually 88 base64 chars ending in ==.

patterns.name: GitHub Personal Access Token (classic)
patterns.description: GitHub classic personal access token: ghp_ prefix plus 36 alphanumeric chars. Related: gho_ (OAuth), ghu_ (user-to-server), ghs_ (server), ghr_ (refresh).
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: Distinctive prefix; GitHub tokens also carry an internal CRC32 checksum that can be validated for certainty.

patterns.name: GitHub fine-grained PAT
patterns.description: GitHub fine-grained personal access token: github_pat_ prefix plus 82 chars (22-char ID, underscore, 59-char secret).
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: Very specific prefix and fixed total length; effectively no false positives.

patterns.name: GitHub OAuth/app token (gho/ghu/ghs/ghr)
patterns.description: GitHub OAuth access (gho_), user-to-server (ghu_), server-to-server (ghs_), and refresh (ghr_) tokens: prefix plus 36 alphanumeric chars.
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: Distinctive prefixes; same CRC32 checksum scheme as classic PATs.

patterns.name: GitLab personal access token
patterns.description: GitLab personal access token with the glpat- prefix; related runner/deploy tokens use glrt-, gldt- etc.
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: Distinctive prefix. Newer GitLab tokens may be longer than 20 chars, hence the open-ended quantifier.

patterns.name: Slack bot token (xoxb)
patterns.description: Slack bot user OAuth token: xoxb- prefix with numeric workspace/bot segments and an alphanumeric secret.
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: Distinctive structure; near-zero false positives. Slack token lengths vary slightly across workspaces.

patterns.name: Slack user token (xoxp)
patterns.description: Slack user OAuth token: xoxp- prefix with three numeric segments and a 32-char hex-like secret.
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: Distinctive structure. Also consider xoxa- (app) and xoxr- (refresh) variants if broader coverage is needed.

patterns.name: OpenAI API key (sk-)
patterns.description: Legacy OpenAI API key: sk- prefix plus 48 alphanumeric chars.
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: The bare sk- prefix is also used by Stripe (sk_live/sk_test use an underscore, so no collision) but a context-free 48-char match can occasionally hit other vendor keys; prefer the more specific sk-proj/sk-ant patterns where applicable.

patterns.name: OpenAI project API key (sk-proj-)
patterns.description: OpenAI project-scoped API key: sk-proj- prefix followed by 48 or more url-safe chars.
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: Distinctive prefix; open-ended length accommodates newer longer keys. Also covers sk-svcacct- style by adjusting prefix if desired.

patterns.name: Anthropic API key (sk-ant-)
patterns.description: Anthropic Claude API key: sk-ant- prefix, key-type segment (api03 standard, admin01 admin), 93 url-safe chars, and a trailing AA.
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: The literal 'sk-ant-api/admin' prefix is unique to Anthropic; a 20+ char tail catches both full and pasted-partial keys with near-zero false positives.

patterns.name: Stripe live secret key (sk_live_)
patterns.description: Stripe live-mode secret API key with the sk_live_ prefix.
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: Distinctive prefix. sk_test_ (test mode) is lower severity. Newer Stripe keys can be substantially longer, hence open-ended quantifier.

patterns.name: Stripe live restricted key (rk_live_)
patterns.description: Stripe live-mode restricted API key with the rk_live_ prefix.
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: Distinctive prefix; restricted keys have scoped permissions but are still secrets. rk_test_ is lower severity.

patterns.name: Twilio Account SID (AC)
patterns.description: Twilio Account SID: AC prefix plus 32 hex chars. Sensitive when paired with the auth token.
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: AC + 32 hex can collide with other 34-char identifiers; pair with an auth-token or API-key match for confidence. The SID alone is not a secret but is PII-adjacent.

patterns.name: Twilio API Key SID (SK)
patterns.description: Twilio API Key SID: SK prefix plus 32 hex chars, typically used with a secret.
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: SK + 32 hex overlaps with other hex identifiers; treat as a lead and correlate with a nearby secret value.

patterns.name: SendGrid API key (SG.)
patterns.description: SendGrid API key: SG. prefix, a 22-char ID segment, a dot, and a 43-char secret segment.
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: Distinctive prefix and segment lengths; very few false positives.

patterns.name: JSON Web Token (JWT)
patterns.description: JWT: three base64url segments separated by dots, where header and payload both begin with eyJ (base64 of {"...).
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: Matches any JWT including non-sensitive/expired ones. Severity depends on contents (access tokens vs public id-tokens). Decode to assess. Unsigned alg=none tokens still match.

patterns.name: PEM private key block
patterns.description: Header of a PEM-encoded private key of any common type (RSA, EC, DSA, OpenSSH, PGP, PKCS#8, encrypted).
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: The header is unambiguous. Documentation and test fixtures can contain placeholder keys; the presence of the header alone warrants action.

patterns.name: OpenSSH private key
patterns.description: Header of an OpenSSH-format private key file (id_ed25519, id_rsa with new format).
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: Unambiguous header; subset of the broader PEM private-key detector, broken out for clearer alerting.

patterns.name: Generic high-entropy assigned secret
patterns.description: A secret-like keyword followed by an assignment and a 16-64 char value; gate on Shannon entropy to keep only random-looking values.
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: Without entropy gating this flags ordinary config values (password=changeme, token=TODO). Shannon entropy threshold (~3.5+ bits/char) is essential. Tune keyword list to your stack.

patterns.name: URL with embedded basic-auth credentials
patterns.description: Any URI scheme carrying user:password@host inline (https, ftp, ssh, git, smtp, etc.).
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: Matches URLs that merely contain a colon before @ even if not real creds (rare). Will not match if username/password contain @ or / (uncommon, should be percent-encoded). Redact the password component in output.

patterns.name: Password in connection string
patterns.description: ADO/JDBC/ODBC-style connection string fragment exposing Password=... or Pwd=...
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: Matches placeholder values too (Password=yourpassword). Combine with surrounding connection-string keys (Server=, Data Source=) for confidence and apply entropy where possible.

patterns.name: Email address
patterns.description: RFC-pragmatic email address: local part, @, domain, and a 2-24 char TLD.
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: Matches example/test domains and code identifiers like user@host in docs. Does not handle quoted local parts or new long TLDs beyond 24 chars. Allowlist internal domains to reduce noise.

patterns.name: US/NANP phone number
patterns.description: North American Numbering Plan phone number with NANP constraints (area and exchange codes start 2-9), optional +1 and common separators.
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: 10-digit number-like strings (IDs, fragments of longer numbers) can match. The 2-9 constraints on the first digits help. May partial-match inside longer digit runs without stricter anchoring.

patterns.name: International phone (E.164)
patterns.description: E.164 international phone number: leading +, country-code first digit 1-9, total 8-15 digits.
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: Will match any +-prefixed 8-15 digit run, including some account/order references written that way. The leading + reduces false positives versus bare digit strings.

patterns.name: Korean mobile phone (010)
patterns.description: South Korean mobile number: 010/011/016/017/018/019 prefix with 3-4 middle digits and 4 final digits.
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: Undashed forms (01012345678) can collide with other 10-11 digit IDs. The 01[016789] prefix is fairly distinctive within KR context.

patterns.name: IPv4 address
patterns.description: Dotted-quad IPv4 address with each octet bounded to 0-255.
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: Version strings (e.g. 10.0.0.1 as a software version) and private/reserved ranges match; classify private vs public downstream. Octet bounding prevents matching invalid like 999.1.1.1.

patterns.name: IPv6 address (full form)
patterns.description: Fully expanded IPv6 address: eight hextet groups separated by colons.
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: Does not match :: compressed/abbreviated forms or embedded IPv4 suffixes; a separate compressed-form pattern is needed for full coverage. MAC-like hex with colons could partially resemble but group count differs.

patterns.name: MAC address
patterns.description: IEEE 802 MAC address: six colon- or dash-separated hex octets.
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: Colon-separated hex of this exact shape is almost always a MAC, but some truncated hashes/UUID fragments formatted similarly could collide. Cisco dotted-triple format (xxxx.xxxx.xxxx) is not matched.

patterns.name: US street address (heuristic)
patterns.description: Heuristic US street address: house number, 1-4 street-name words, and a common street-type suffix.
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: Heuristic only; prose containing a number plus 'Way'/'Dr'/'St' can false-match. No ZIP/city validation. Best used as a weak signal combined with other address components.

patterns.name: ICD-10 diagnosis code
patterns.description: ICD-10(-CM) diagnosis code: letter (excluding U historically), two characters, optional decimal extension.
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: Short alphanumeric codes collide heavily with non-medical tokens (cell refs like E11, IDs). Strong medical context required. The decimal extension form is the most distinctive; consider requiring it.

patterns.name: US National Provider Identifier (NPI)
patterns.description: US healthcare NPI: 10 digits starting with 1 or 2, validated with the Luhn algorithm over the prefixed constant 80840.
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: Plain 10-digit numbers are common; validate using Luhn with the '80840' prefix per CMS spec to confirm. Without that check, false-positive rate is high.

patterns.name: US DEA registration number
patterns.description: US DEA controlled-substance registrant number: registrant-type letter, a second letter (registrant last-name initial) or 9, then 7 digits with a checksum in the last digit.
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: The last digit is a checksum ((sum of 1st,3rd,5th) + 2*(sum of 2nd,4th,6th)) mod 10; validate downstream. Two-letter + 7-digit tokens can otherwise collide with part numbers.

patterns.name: Database connection URL (.env)
patterns.description: Database/broker connection URI as commonly found in .env files, often embedding user:password@host credentials.
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: Matches credential-free URLs too (e.g. redis://localhost). Severity rises when a basic-auth userinfo segment is present; correlate with the basic-auth-url pattern.

patterns.name: JDBC connection URL
patterns.description: Java JDBC connection string for any driver, frequently carrying user/password query parameters.
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: Matches credential-free JDBC URLs as well; elevate when a password= parameter is present. The trailing greedy match stops at whitespace/quotes.

patterns.name: Slack incoming webhook URL
patterns.description: Slack incoming webhook URL containing the team (T...), bot (B...), and secret token path segments.
patterns.falsePositiveNotes: Fixed host and path structure make this very specific; the final segment is the secret and must be redacted.
